questions.question: If sin(θ) = 3/5 and π/2 < θ < π, find the value of tan(θ).
questions.explanation: Since sin(θ) = 3/5 and θ is in Quadrant II, use sin²(θ) + cos²(θ) = 1 to get cos²(θ) = 1 - 9/25 = 16/25. In Quadrant II, cos(θ) < 0, so cos(θ) = -4/5. Then tan(θ) = sin(θ)/cos(θ) = (3/5)/(-4/5) = -3/4.

questions.question: Simplify: (sin²(α) - cos²(α))/(sin(α) - cos(α)). Express your answer as a single trigonometric expression.
questions.explanation: Factor the numerator: sin²(α) - cos²(α) = (sin(α) - cos(α))(sin(α) + cos(α)). Cancel the common factor (sin(α) - cos(α)) from numerator and denominator to get sin(α) + cos(α).

questions.question: A right triangle has legs of length 7 and 24. If θ is the acute angle opposite the side of length 7, what is sin(θ) + cos(θ)?
questions.explanation: The hypotenuse is √(7² + 24²) = √(49 + 576) = √625 = 25. Then sin(θ) = 7/25 (opposite/hypotenuse) and cos(θ) = 24/25 (adjacent/hypotenuse). Thus sin(θ) + cos(θ) = 7/25 + 24/25 = 31/25.

questions.question: If tan(β) = 5/12 and β is in Quadrant I, find sin(β) - cos(β).
questions.explanation: From tan(β) = 5/12, construct a right triangle with opposite = 5, adjacent = 12, hypotenuse = 13. Then sin(β) = 5/13 and cos(β) = 12/13. So sin(β) - cos(β) = 5/13 - 12/13 = -7/13.

questions.question: Verify the identity: (1 - sin²(x))·sec(x) = cos(x). Show your work with one intermediate step.
questions.explanation: Use the Pythagorean identity 1 - sin²(x) = cos²(x). Then cos²(x)·sec(x) = cos²(x)·(1/cos(x)) = cos(x).

questions.question: On the unit circle, a point P at angle θ has coordinates (−5/13, 12/13). Without finding θ explicitly, determine cos(2θ).
